coredata.dc:title: Exploring the effects of aging, temperature and hysteresis on the entropy variation of lithium-ion batteries

coredata.dc:description:
The entropy variation
Δ S
poses a fundamental characteristic of lithium-ion batteries due to its influence on their thermal behavior. This study presents an extensive open source dataset containing potentiometric entropy variation measurements on two commercially available cell types. The experimental campaign includes 20 temperature steps over 9 different states of charge (SoC) approached in charging and discharging direction. Further measurements on half cell level were conducted in order to investigate the electrodes’ contribution to the full cell entropy. In addition, a reduced testing regime was applied to 6 degraded cells aged via various aging regimes. The measurements were analyzed to quantify the influence of SoC, non-linearity, hysteresis, temperature-path-dependency and aging on the entropy variation. Comparing the results to literature, the expected influence of both SoC and load-history-dependent hysteresis were confirmed. Regarding the influence of non-linearity, no significant effect was determined for the given dataset. The effects temperature-path-dependency observed in literature were successfully reproduced. However, the proposed loop-like relationship resembling common hysteresis phenomena could not be verified. The reconstruction of the full cell entropy variation curve from half cell measurements was successfully conducted for cells in pristine condition, while its application on aged specimens proved difficult likely due to challenges during determination of the electrode balancing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The entropy variation
Δ S
poses a fundamental characteristic of lithium-ion batteries due to its influence on their thermal behavior. This study presents an extensive open source dataset containing potentiometric entropy variation measurements on two commercially available cell types. The experimental campaign includes 20 temperature steps over 9 different states of charge (SoC) approached in charging and discharging direction. Further measurements on half cell level were conducted in order to investigate the electrodes’ contribution to the full cell entropy. In addition, a reduced testing regime was applied to 6 degraded cells aged via various aging regimes. The measurements were analyzed to quantify the influence of SoC, non-linearity, hysteresis, temperature-path-dependency and aging on the entropy variation. Comparing the results to literature, the expected influence of both SoC and load-history-dependent hysteresis were confirmed. Regarding the influence of non-linearity, no significant effect was determined for the given dataset. The effects temperature-path-dependency observed in literature were successfully reproduced. However, the proposed loop-like relationship resembling common hysteresis phenomena could not be verified. The reconstruction of the full cell entropy variation curve from half cell measurements was successfully conducted for cells in pristine condition, while its application on aged specimens proved difficult likely due to challenges during determination of the electrode balancing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data presented in this study can be accessed online under the following link: https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/6X5TF .

ce:section.ce:para:
Understanding the thermal behavior of lithium-ion batteries has proven essential due to its influence on a battery’s electrical and aging behavior as well as its operational safety [1–4]. Within the thermal domain, the entropy variation
Δ S
represents a fundamental characteristic owing to its associated heat generation
Q
̇
entropy
. Eq. (1) describes the entropy heat generation in dependence of the cell temperature
T
cell
, the load current
I
cell
, the Faraday constant F and the entropy variation
Δ S
[5]:
(1)
Q
̇
entropy
=
I
cell
⋅
T
cell
⋅
Δ S
F
=
I
cell
⋅
T
cell
⋅
∂
V
eq
∂
T
cell

Applying the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation, the quotient
Δ S / F
can also be expressed as the partial derivative of the equilibrium voltage
V
eq
with respect to temperature
∂
V

/ ∂
T
cell
. Assuming ideal relaxation,
V
eq
equals the open-circuit voltage (OCV). The expression
Δ S / F = ∂
V

/ ∂
T
cell
≊ ∂ O C V / ∂
T
cell
is commonly also referred to as the entropy heat coefficient (EHC). Analogous to the OCV of a battery
O C
V
cell
, which is determined by the potential difference between the two individual electrodes
O C
V
cathode
and
O C
V
anode
, the entropy variation can also be calculated by subtracting the two half cell entropy variations
Δ
S
cathode
and
Δ
S

[6,7]:
(2)

S

= Δ
S

S

Several methods for determining a battery’s entropy variation have been proposed. The predominant so-called potentiometric approach measures OCV changes while altering a cells’ temperature assuming
∂ O C V / ∂ T ≊ Δ O C V / Δ T
. Most commonly, the cell temperature is varied via a step-wise change of the ambient temperature leading to an approximately first-order cell temperature response [7–20]. Alternatively, Hu et al. [21] adapted the potentiometric approach by applying a sinusoidal temperature excitation and measuring a sinusoidal voltage response. Mertin et al. [22] derived an analytical relation between the exponential temperature slope and the resulting variation of the OCV allowing the computation of the EHC during thermal relaxation. Apart from the potentiometric approach, the entropy variation can also be determined via calorimetric measurements i.e. the determination of the heat generation
Q
̇
entropy
. Usually, this is achieved by charging and discharging a battery within a calorimeter and isolating the entropy heat from the irreversible components [9,17,23]. Geng et al. [24] and Schmidt et al. [25] applied calorimetric thermal impedance spectroscopy in order to obtain the temperature response to a current excitation. Damay et al. [26,27] replaced the calorimeter by a heat dissipation model allowing the model-based determination of the entropy heat during a single full discharge and charge cycle. Bedürftig et al. [28] measured the temperature response from load pulses in charge and discharge direction.

Analogous to the OCV, battery entropy variation also depends on the thermodynamic activities of the electrode materials and therefore also exhibits a pronounced dependency on the state of charge (SoC) [27–29]. The full cell entropy variation curve is determined by the individual electrode entropy variations and the stoichiometry of the cell also referred to as the electrode balancing. The electrode balancing reflects the relative capacities and states of charge of the individual electrodes compared to the common full cell capacity and SoC axis [30]. Joining unsymmetrical anode and cathode capacities is common practice in battery cell design [30].

Over time and usage, lithium-ion batteries experience degradation, also known as calendar and cycle aging. Birkl et al. [31] among others cluster the effects of the various existing degradation mechanisms into three degradation modes: Loss of lithium inventory (LLI), loss of active anode material (Anode-LAM) and loss of active cathode material (Cathode-LAM). The different modes alter the electrode balancing subsequently affecting the OCV-SoC and EHC-SoC relationship by shifting or skewing their distinct features [32]. Differential voltage analysis (DVA) enables quantification of these alterations by analyzing the distinct extrema in the
∂ O C V / ∂ SoC
(dOCV) curve of the electrodes and the full cell [33]. Osswald et al. [15] measured the effects of cycle and calendar aging on the EHC indicating a significant shift of the distinctive peaks of the EHC curve for both aging modes. Spitthoff et al. [8] and Hu et al. [21] determined the EHC curve for cells at various states of health (SoH). Yun et al. [13] measured both the variation of the full cell EHC-SoC curve with aging and the changes in the electrode EHC curves over their lithiation states. The results indicate that in addition to altering the electrode balancing, aging does also effect the entropy variation of the individual electrodes, though to a lesser extent [13]. Kunz et al. [23] reconstruct the full cell EHC curve from the electrode EHC curves in order to determine and distinguish degradation modes.

In addition to its dependency on the SoC and aging state, some researchers also report a pronounced non-linearity of the entropy variation i.e.
∂ O C V / ∂
T
cell
itself being a function of temperature. Bazinski and Wang [16] determined the EHC over the entire usable temperature and SoC range, finding a significant negative dependency at the SoC extrema. Hu et al. [21] determined the entropy variation over five different temperatures concluding an increase of the absolute value with rising temperatures. Takano et al. [20] tested at three temperature levels reporting varying results over different cell samples and SoCs. Mertin et al. [29] adapted Fermi–Dirac statistics for an analytical description of the phenomenon resulting in a parabolic dependency of the OCV on the temperature i.e. a linear relation between EHC and temperature [29]. The proposed relation was validated over 13 temperatures.

Hysteresis commonly refers to the residual difference between the charge and discharge voltage curve of a battery while approaching infinitesimally small currents [34]. Regarding lithium-ion batteries, hysteresis is usually considered the result of first-order phase transitions in the active material particles [35]. As introduced by Dreyer et al. [34], hysteresis requires particles with a non-monotonic open-circuit potential curve allowing two-phase decomposition. In a multi-particle system, this enables particle-by-particle charging leading to thermodynamically stable voltage hysteresis. A mechanistic modeling approach to this phenomenon was introduced by Jahn et al. [36]. As derived by Mertin et al. [6], the existence of OCV hysteresis also allows the existence of entropy hysteresis i.e. a difference between the entropy variation at a given SoC when approached in charging or discharging direction [6]. Tardy et al. [37], Schmidt et al. [25] and Bedürftig et al. [28] confirm this effect for various different cell types. Takano et al. [20] proposes self-discharge and a resulting distortion of the SoC grid as a potential reason for entropy hysteresis. However, with modern Li-ion batteries achieving
> 99
%
coulombic efficiency, this effect should only account for a minor share of the visible entropy hysteresis.

While a dependence of
Δ S
on the load history is well documented, deviations caused by temperature-path-dependency are yet rarely reported in literature. Measurements conducted by Zilberman et al. [12] indicate that positive and negative temperature steps on cells in electrical equilibrium lead to different OCVs when approaching a common temperature. Consequently, deviations of the EHC in the double digit percentage were observed. Zilberman et al. characterize the observed effect as a temperature-path-dependent voltage hysteresis. Similar observations were made by Takano et al. [20]. However, they assume ongoing electro-thermal relaxation processes such as remaining thermal gradients within the cell after reaching thermal equilibrium on the surface to be responsible. Therefore, whether a significant temperature-path-dependency exists and, if so, takes the form of a loop-like hysteresis phenomenon remains yet unconfirmed.

Based on the presented literature, this study provides an exhaustive open source dataset including potentiometric measurement data for two cell types with different form factors, allowing the investigation of the reported influences. Considering SoC-dependency, non-linearity and load-history-dependent entropy hysteresis, the observations given in common literature are tested on the dataset. Regarding the influence of aging, the study tests the hypothesis that changes in the EHC-SoC relationship of aged cells are primarily due to changes in the electrode balancing. In this context, the consequences of different aging regimes leading to different variations of the electrode balancing are also investigated. On the proposed temperature-path-dependency, this study attempts to reproduce the observations made by Zilberman et al. [12] including a thorough investigation of the thermal relaxation processes to differentiate ongoing relaxation from actual path-dependency.
In order to access the dataset accompanying this study, readers may refer to the data availability statement.

During the investigations, two commercially available cell types, hereinafter referred to as Type A and Type B, were tested. Type A is a cylindrical cell in 21,700-format with lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide (NCA) and silicon-graphite (SiC) as active materials providing a nominal capacity of 4.9 Ah. In the case of Type A, nine specimens (A-1 to A-9) from the same production batch were used during testing. While cells A-1 to A-3 were in pristine condition at beginning of testing, cells A-4 to A-9 were explicitly aged according to specific aging regimes introduced in Section 2.2. Type B is a pouch-type cell with lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) and graphite as active materials providing 64 Ah of coulombic capacity. Further characteristics of both cell types are given in Table 1 . During testing, Cell B was clamped between two 2.3 mm steel sheets after applying an external prestress of 2 kN.

To perform further electrode-specific measurements, coin half cells in CR2032 format were manufactured from both cell types according to the procedure given in [38]. As active materials, 16 mm single-side coated cut-outs of the respective electrode were harvested. As counter-electrode, a metal lithium chip separated via a 17 mm Whatman GC/F separator was inserted. The full setup was saturated in
90
μ l
of 1 mol/l
LiPF
6
(EC:EMC 30:70 wt %) electrolyte. In total, 6 half cells per electrode with a nominal capacity of 8.3 mAh (Type A) and 4.4 mAh (Type B) were manufactured. During the EHC measurements, the half cells with the highest coulombic efficiency
η
C
=
C
dch
/
C
cha
as obtained from the OCV measurements were used.

All electrical measurements in the time domain were conducted on a Battery Dynamics HRT M-Series tester that provided a calibrated accuracy of
± 6 . 6
μ A
and
± 11
μ V
in the used measurement range. For electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements, a Zahner Zennium Pro potentiostat with an accuracy of 2 % amplitude and 3° phase in the relevant measurement range was used. All measurements were conducted in a Binder KT53 climate chamber. The cell surface temperature of each cell was captured by a NTC-type temperature sensor placed on the cells’ surface and thermally insulated against the environment via a
1
cm
2
styrofoam pad. The ambient temperature in the chamber was further captured with a separate PT100-type temperature sensor. All temperature sensors were connected to the Battery Dynamics tester. In order to quantify the achievable temperature measurement accuracy, all sensors were tested against a Lufft XP100 reference sensor in a distilled watherbath at 10, 20, 30 and 40 °C resulting in a maximum offset error of 0.2 K (cell surface) and 0.9 K (ambient) as well as a maximum gradient error of 1.0 % (cell surface) and 2.5 % (ambient).

Cells A-4 to A-9 were aged as part of a comprehensive multi-stage battery degradation study described in [39]. To validate optimal experimental design methodologies [40,41], 279 individual cells were subjected to 79 distinct aging conditions. In the calendar aging part of the study, cells were stored in open-loop condition at a specified state of charge and ambient temperature (
T
amb
) for a duration of approximately one year each. The cycle aging part involved continuous charge–discharge cycles at specified charge and discharge rates (
I
cha
,
I
dch
) under controlled ambient temperature conditions. Each cycling phase started at a maximum state of charge (
SoC
max
). The cells were then discharged until reaching the target depth of discharge (
D o D
), at which point the corresponding cell voltage was recorded and established as the lower bound voltage for subsequent cycling. During the following cycles, the charging process was controlled through ampere-hour counting until reaching the specified DoD, after which the cell was discharged to the previously defined lower bound voltage.

Throughout both calendar and cycle aging campaigns, reference performance tests (RPTs) were conducted at regular intervals to characterize the cells aging states. The characterization protocols were modified throughout the study to minimize the impact of RPTs on the primary aging regimes. The concluding step in each RPT involved charging the cell to the selected
SoC
max
. The full data set is available at [42], the cells used in this study are given in Table 2 .

Prior to the entropy measurement campaign, thermal relaxation measurements on each cell type with the purpose of determining their thermal time constants
τ
th
were conducted. During these measurements one cell of Type A and Type B each as well as one half cell specimen manufactured from a Type B cell underwent a temperature step from 15 to 5 °C with a subsequent 24 h waiting period. The negative temperature step was chosen as the used climate chamber applies Peltier elements for cooling, which induce significantly longer time constants compared to resistive heating. Therefore, the cooling scenario must be considered as the worst (slowest) case. 15 to 5 °C represents the lowest temperature step applied during the entropy measurement campaign (see Section 2.5).

Fig. 1 shows the resulting ambient and cell surface temperatures recorded during the measurement. However, especially for large-format cells, the surface temperature is not a sufficient surrogate for the thermal relaxation behavior of the whole cell. For this reason, additional impedance measurements on all cells were conducted during the test. The obtained impedance values show a significant non-linear dependency on the cells’ temperature and are therefore suitable for numerically determining the mean thermal relaxation behavior (see Section 3.1). All impedance measurements were conducted minute-wise at a frequency of 1 Hz over 10 periods. This frequency was chosen as previous investigations showed that the imaginary component of the 1 Hz impedance exhibits a pronounced temperature dependency for both cell types. The excitation was achieved via sinusoidal currents of C/32 for each cell, i.e. 0.153 A (Type A), 2 A (Type B) and 0.1375 mA (half cell). The resulting trajectory of the imaginary impedance component of cell B is also shown in Fig. 1.

In order to determine the capacity and OCV curve of the specimens before, during and after the entropy variation tests, constant current (CC) measurements with a rate of C/50 were conducted. The pseudo-OCV measurement regime includes an initial charging cycle to the maximum cell voltage followed by a charge–discharge sequence between the minimum and maximum voltage as given in Table 1. All measurements were conducted at 20 °C. To track the unintended aging progress of cells A-1 to A-3 and cell B during the entropy variation tests, the OCV measurements were repeated after half of the scheduled measurements (index mid) as well as after concluding the entropy variation measurement campaign (index end). The purposely aged cells A-4 to A-9 were not subjected to additional OCV measurements during the entropy variation campaign, as they underwent a heavily shortened testing matrix (see Section 2.4). Fig. 2 shows the voltage trajectory during discharging of the two cell types as well as their derivatives to the SoC. The corresponding capacities normalized to their nominal values are given in Table 3 .

The described pseudo-OCV measurement regime was also applied to the half cells obtained from both cell types. Cathode half cells were cycled between 2.7 V and 4.35 V, anode half cells between 0.01 V and 1.5 V. The resulting half cell OCV and differential OCV curves obtained in charging direction are given in Fig. 3 .

During the entropy variation tests, cells A-1 to A-3 as well as cell B were subjected to the full-scale measurement matrix, which includes 20 ambient temperature steps per SoC step. Fig. 4 (a) shows the resulting surface temperature trajectory for cell A-1 at 50 % SoC approached in charging direction. The temperature steps are grouped around four distinct temperature levels (15, 25, 35 and 45 °C) with each temperature level being approached from both a positive and negative temperature delta of
± 10
K allowing the investigation of temperature path-dependency. Each ambient temperature step is held for a relaxation period of 12 h resulting in a total measurement duration of 252 h per SoC step. Further analysis of the required relaxation times is given in Section 3.1. Fig. 4(b) shows the resulting voltage response of the cell to the ambient temperature excitation.

The introduced temperature profile is applied at eleven different SoCs between 10 % and 90 % in 10 % steps. Each SoC is newly approached from the fully charged or discharged state with a constant current of C/50. By this procedure, the accumulation of self-discharge effects over multiple SoC steps is prevented. After reaching the desired SoC, the cell is relaxed for 12 h before initiating the shown temperature variation. The entire measurement matrix is conducted both in charge and discharge direction i.e. the desired SoCs are approached both from the fully charged and fully discharged state. As a consequence, the complete entropy variation regime amounts to 5544 h or eight months of testing.

In contrast to the full-scale procedure, cells A-4 to A-9 as well as the half cells were subjected to a reduced measurement matrix with one temperature step from 15 °C to 25 °C per SoC applying the same 12 h relaxation time per step for the aged cells. For the half cells, the relaxation time was reduced to four hours in order to limit the influence of the increased self-discharge of the half cells. During both the half cell tests and the measurements on the aged specimens, 19 SoCs between 5 % and 95 % in 5 % steps were approached. The finer SoC grid was chosen to enable a better identification of the distinct features of the EHC-SoC curve. For the aged and half cells, each SoC was approached directly after the previous SoC step without prior discharge. This was deemed acceptable due to the heavily reduced testing time. Fig. 5 shows the voltage trajectory obtained from testing the cathode half cell of cell type A.

In order to determine the thermal time constants from the thermal relaxation measurements shown in Section 2.3, numerical optimization was used. Assuming a homogeneous temperature distribution within the cell, the 0D thermal model given in Eq. (3) describes the mean cell temperature depending on its heat capacity
C
th
and its thermal resistance
R
th
. Since the mean temperature within a cell cannot be directly measured, the relationship between its impedance Z and temperature can be used to determine the mean thermal relaxation behavior. Eq. (4) describes the Arrhenius-relation between impedance and temperature as given by Spinner et al. [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential voltage analysis and electrode balancing
ce:section.ce:para:
As introduced in Section 1, the differential open-circuit voltage
∂ O C V / ∂ SoC
can be used for the analysis of the thermodynamic characteristics of a cell and how they are influenced by different degradation modes. Fig. 2 shows the differential voltage trajectory of both cell types. The visible peaks in the trajectories can be assigned to specific lattice configurations and stages between phase transitions. Separation of different stages requires knowledge about the half cell potential curves as given in Figs. 3(b) & (e). Fig. 2(a) shows the specific markers for the silicon (Si-1, Si-2) and graphite (C-4L, C-3L, C-2L, C-2) compound in A-type anodes [43]. The local maxima indicating the monoclinic (M) and hexagonal (H2) compound in the NCA cathode are also clearly visible [48]. Fig. 2(b) shows the results for B-type cells with comparable markers for the graphite anode but no significant extrema on the NMC cathode side, both at full cell and half cell level.

Instead of comparing the half and full cell OCV, the outlined optimization approach can also be adopted to use
∂ O C V / ∂ SoC
. This can be helpful since the differential OCV trajectory often exhibits more pronounced extrema compared to the OCV. During this study, the electrode balancing of A-type cells was determined using the differential OCV. The results of the electrode balancing using
O C
V
mid
in charging direction are shown in Figs. 3(b) and (e).

Fig. 4(b) shows one of the voltage trajectories measured during the entropy variation campaign. Although the total measurement takes 252 h, an ongoing voltage drift is clearly visible. Applying the thermal time constants obtained in Section 3.1 and assuming full thermal relaxation after 10 time constants, all remaining movement after approx. 3 h (Type A), 6 h (Type B) or 2 h (half cell) of every temperature step must be considered to be of electrical origin. This voltage drift might be due to a) ongoing electrical relaxation or (b) self-discharge (both internally and over the connected test rig). As these voltage drifts distort the OCV-temperature relationship which is used to characterize the entropy variation, further correction is usually applied [7,12,15,21,28].

Assuming sufficient preceding relaxation, both the remaining relaxation effects and self-discharge can be linearized with acceptable fault [50,51]. This assumption is modeled by Eq. (10) for the k th temperature step. In order to obtain the drift gradient

As can be seen in Fig. 5, the voltage trajectories obtained during testing of the aged cells and the half cell specimens still show a pronounced curvature even after full thermal relaxation (6 h for aged cells, 2 h for half cells) which is caused by the heavily reduced total relaxation time after each SoC step (24 h/8 h vs. 252 h). To address this problem, Eq. (10) is extended by an exponential term with a single relaxation time constant:

0 , k = 1
can be directly used as a surrogate for the thermally relaxed voltage after the temperature step. Fig. 5 shows the fitted relaxation and self-discharge curves for one SoC step.

Figs. 6 and 7
show the results of the entropy variation measurement campaign on full cell level. The OCV vs. temperature trajectories obtained from the different temperature steps after applying drift correction are given in Fig. 6 for three SoCs (10 %, 50 %, 90 %) approached in charging and discharging direction. The inter-cell variations between cells A-1, A-2, and A-3 are visualized as error bars. In the following, A-type results refer to the average value of the three specimens. The linear regressions over all temperature steps of one operating point are plotted as dashed lines with the corresponding slope values given beside.

Fig. 7 shows the EHC-SoC trajectories obtained in charging and discharging direction for both cell types. The filled areas contain all EHC values that can be calculated from the 19 individual temperatures steps. The trajectories for two specific temperature steps (square marker: 35 → 25 °C, diamond marker: 15 → 25 °C as given in Fig. 4) are plotted individually.

The gradients of the linear regressions shown in Fig. 6 are equal to the average entropy heat coefficients of the respective operating points. The average deviation between the two regressions in charging and discharging direction equals the average OCV hysteresis at that SoC. The difference of the gradients of both regressions equals the average load-history-dependent entropy hysteresis. Regarding the SoC-dependency of the entropy variation, the results exhibit strong negative values towards smaller SoCs and a pronounced positive plateau between 40–60 % SoC for both cell types. In terms of load-history-dependent hysteresis, a significant difference between SoC steps approached in charging and discharging direction is visible for both cell types (see Figs. 7(a) and (b)). This dependency is particularly apparent for SoCs between 30 and 70 % while the extrema are relatively unaffected. Remarkably, neither the charge nor discharge curve is consistently above or below the other curve. Instead, the discharging curve exceeds the charging curve between 30 and 40 % SoC.

In order to analyze the non-linearity of the EHC, the coefficient of determination
R
2
was calculated for all operating points using a linear and a parabolic correlation as formulated in [29]. The upper half of Table 5 lists
R
2
applying linear (top value in each cell) and parabolic (bottom value in each cell) regression for all SoC steps, directions and cell types. On average, using a parabolic fit yields a higher degree of determination compared to linear regression (84.24 % to 84.88 %). In the lower half of Table 5, the adjusted coefficient of determination
R
¯
2
as proposed by Ezekiel [52] is given for the same operating points. The adjusted coefficient of determination considers the number of explanatory parameters p in comparison to the number of measured samples n :
(14)
R
¯
2
= 1 −
( 1 −
R
2
)
n − 1
n − p − 1

Using
R
¯
2
, the systematic improvement of the fit of a function with the number of free parameters independent of the quality of the actual correlation can be compensated for. The calculated
R
¯
2
values for the dataset show that the average improvement achieved by assuming a parabolic instead of a linear relation is significantly decreased and even turns negative to 83.41 % vs. 83.34 % on average. This also applies if operating points with particularly low coefficients of determination (values below 80 % colored in red in Table 5) are neglected in the mean (95.68 to 95.66 %). Particularly low coefficients of determination occur at operating points with (a) significant temperature-path-dependency (see Section 4.1.3) or (b) near-zero OCV changes rendering the procedure more susceptible to overfitting (e.g. Type B results over 70 % SoC).

The results given in Fig. 7 show that the extent of the entropy variation obtained from the main entropy measurement campaign can depend heavily on the temperature step chosen for investigation. These results suggest a relevant temperature-path-dependency for certain SoCs and load histories. For further analysis, two operating points (30 % SoC approached in charging direction for A-type cells, 30 % SoC approached in discharging direction of B-type cells) are investigated in Fig. 8 . Figs. 8(a) and (c) show the corresponding voltage trajectories before and after drift correction normalized to the voltage of the first shown temperature step. For both cell types, it can be seen that the cell voltage does not relax toward a common equilibrium for positive and negative temperature steps. Looking at the 15 °C to 25 °C i.e. the 25 °C to 15 °C temperature step, the observed voltage response differs by more than 0.7 mV. It is also noteworthy that the observed difference seemingly does not build up or regress over the full duration but is fully formed within the early phases of the temperature step excitation.

The OCV vs. temperature plots obtained from these measurements are given in Fig. 8(b)–(d). The colored arrowheads indicate the order of the temperature steps along the test regime. It can be seen that for heating and cooling paths, the resulting OCV differs by more than 1 mV for the most extreme operating points. This observation is consistent for both cell types. A closing of a potential hysteresis loop, i.e. a re-convergence of the heating path to the cooling path cannot be observed from the data. Rather, the consecutive cooling path is found to be similar to the previous heating path until previously unapproached temperatures are reached.

In order to further investigate the observed phenomenon, two additional measurement campaigns on three specimens (A-1, A-6, A-9) at 30 % SoC approached in charging direction were conducted. For the first test, the available temperature range of 5 °C to 55 °C was approached in consecutive 10 K heating and cooling steps. The test parameters were kept equal to the main entropy measurement campaign and drift correction was applied accordingly. The results are given in Fig. 9 (a) with the order of the temperature steps indicated over the data points. It can be seen that the heating and cooling paths result in two significantly different OCV vs. temperature trajectories with almost 2 mV difference at the 55 °C temperature step. Remarkably, though, the measurement still does not show a significant re-convergence of the two paths to a common equilibrium in the given temperature range.

For the second additional test, the same cells were repeatedly thermally cycled between 5 °C and 25 °C in 10 K temperature steps. Cells A-6 and A-9 experienced five full cooling and heating cycles. For cell A-1 only two cycles were captured as the cell fell out of operation during the test. The corresponding results are given in Fig. 9(b). It can be seen that the extent of the observed path-dependency is similar for the new and aged specimen during the first cycle. However, after conducting the first cycle, the effect regresses significantly with the cells remaining close to the OCV vs. temperature path accessed during the first heating sequence. For cells A-6 and A-9 this can be observed for the remaining four temperature cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Half cell entropy variation and influence of aging
ce:section.ce:para: The half cell EHC vs. SoC trajectories obtained as given in Section 2.4. are shown in Figs. 3(c) and (f). It is clearly visible that the majority of the distinct curvature is contributed by the anode half cell with increasingly positive values towards lower SoCs and a plateau in the medium SoC range. The trajectories of the cathode half cells are less distinctive, especially with respect to cell type B (NMC), exhibiting an almost flat EHC-SoC curve. By applying the electrode balancing parameters obtained from Section 3.2 for the half cell OCV curves, the full cell entropy curve was reconstructed. The resulting simulated full cell EHC curves as well as the measured full cell entropy variations computed from the same 15 to 25 °C temperature step are shown in Figs. 3(c) and (f). The reconstructed and measured curves are generally in good agreement for both cell types, however, Type A results show relevant deviations at the end of the characteristic plateau at 60 % SoC.

As introduced among others by Kunz et al. [23] and Wojtala et al. [45] the distinct features of the half and full cell entropy variation can be related to the corresponding features of the differential OCV curve. This relation is shown in Fig. 3 for the C-4L and C-2 peak of the anode differential OCV.

Figs. 10 (a) and (c) show the measured full cell entropy variation curves obtained from cells A-4 to A-6 and A-7 to A-9, respectively as well as the curves obtained from pristine specimens. Regarding the influence of degradation, cells A-4 to A-6 (
SoH
C,mean
= 95 . 1
%
) exhibit only minimal deviations from the pristine state. Furthermore, the deviation between the three specimens, which underwent three different aging regimes is also negligible. For cells A-7 to A-9 (
SoH
C,mean
= 90 . 1
%
) both the deviations from the pristine measurement and the inter-cell deviation depending on the chosen aging regime are more pronounced. All three cells show a degree of flattening of their distinct features, with cell A-9 (cycle aging, 60 % DoD) exhibiting the largest deviation from the pristine measurements.

The differential OCV curves for the same cells are given in Figs. 10(b) and (d). As the graphs show, the curves change significantly during aging, most prominently visible at the C-2 peak shifting towards higher SoCs. Furthermore, all aged curves show significant flattening and smearing of their distinct features, most pronounced for cell A-9 with its C-2L to C-4L peak becoming indistinguishable and its C-2 peak decreasing.

By applying the methodology introduced in Section 3.2, the electrode balancing of the aged cells was determined and used for the reconstruction of their full cell equivalents from the half cell measurements. Figs. 10(a)–(d) show the reconstructed differential OCV and EHC curves for cells A-6 and A-9. For cell A-6, both the
∂ O C V / ∂ SoC
and the EHC curve could be reconstructed with acceptable accuracy. However, the reconstruction of the EHC of cell A-9 does not represent the flattening observed at full cell level, instead showing a shift of the C-2 flank towards higher SoCs as well as a vanishing of the C-2L to C-4L peak range. The same applies to the reconstruction of the
∂ O C V / ∂ SoC
curve. Although the positions of the peaks were generally matched, the shape of the reconstructed curve differs significantly from the full cell measurement.

This study presents a comprehensive set of potentiometric entropy variation measurements. The SoC dependency and load-history-dependent hysteresis of the entropy variation determined from the data match common literature in both extend and systematics [25,28]. Notable are the pronounced differences between A- and B-type cells, particularly at SoCs beyond 70 %. As shown by the half cell measurements, the anode half cell contributes most of the curvature to the full cell entropy variation, while both the NMC and NCA cathode show relatively small entropy variations.

Regarding the non-linearity of the entropy variation, minimal increases of the coefficient of determination
R
2
can be calculated from the dataset by assuming a parabolic relationship. However, it may be premature to conclude that a relevant non-linearity can be observed, as the use of the adjusted coefficient of determination
R
¯
2
, which considers the number of explanatory parameters, mitigates these improvements. For this reason, the non-linearity shown in the presented literature cannot be confirmed on the dataset. However, this might also be due to the limited temperature range between 5 and 55 °C.

The temperature-path-dependency observed by Zilberman et al. [12] was successfully reproduced for both tested cell types. Based on the data obtained in this study, it was found that the effect is not significant under all operating conditions, but is triggered by specific SoCs and load histories. Ongoing thermal relaxation as a cause for this behavior appears unlikely due to the magnitude of the measured deviations and the small remaining voltage drift. Furthermore, by applying relaxation times of
10 ⋅
τ
th
as determined from thermal relaxation measurements, sufficient relaxation can be expected. In contrast to the results given in [12], the loop-like characteristic of the phenomenon suggesting a hysteresis-type path-dependency could not be observed. Instead, repeated thermal cycling caused convergence towards a common OCV vs. temperature trajectory independent of the path. This behavior was consistent across all tested specimens, regardless of the degradation state. These results also align with the observations made during the main entropy variation campaign given in Fig. 8, where consecutive heating and cooling cycles partially share the same OCV vs. temperature trajectory until previously untested temperature steps are reached. Identifying the exact conditions, under which this effect becomes active, inactive and reactivated between the operation cycles of a cell should therefore be the focus of future studies. Furthermore, investigating the material-level properties of cells subjected to different temperature paths could provide valuable insights into the underlying mechanisms of the observed phenomenon.

The reconstruction of the full cell entropy variation curve from the half cell entropy curves and the electrode balancing obtained from OCV measurements was successful in pristine condition. Apart from measurement or voltage drift correction errors, the remaining deviations (specifically for A-type cells) might also stem from uncertainties of the SoC operating point during half cell EHC measurements, as the half cell specimens show a highly increased self-discharge. Generally, the shown potentiomentric measurement approach has systematic disadvantages compared to other measurement methods due to the limited resolution of the resulting EHC curve. Calorimetric measurements as performed among others by Kunz et al. [23] therefore may yield better results but either require specialized measurement equipment or rely on model assumptions regarding the thermal behavior of the specimen.

In contrast to cells in pristine condition, synthesizing the full cell EHC curve of aged specimens based on the electrode balancing did not allow the accurate reconstruction of the distinct features. One potential reason for this lies in the determination of the electrode balancing of aged cells. As shown in Section 4.2, the aged dOCV curves are subject to significant smearing. Sieg et al. [53] explain this effect by assuming an electrode to consist of multiple particles with differing capacities being electrically connected in parallel. Therefore, when subjected to a constant current experiment, these particles exhibit different particle SoCs reaching their distinct features at different times. The half cell OCV as a superposition of all particles therefore appears smeared. The effect can be compared to the behavior of parallel-connected cells experiencing cell-to-cell variations [54]. In addition, smearing can also be caused when the particles themselves share a similar total capacity but the relation between anode and cathode capacity varies among the particles, e.g. due to local degradation. Smearing poses a relevant challenge when determining the electrode balancing, as the distinctive features relevant for fitting become less apparent.

Further challenges when determining the electrode balancing of aged cells may arise, if those cells use composite electrodes. This effect is specifically evident for silicon-graphite anodes, which often separate in distinct silicon and graphite particles with their own respective OCV curves. This effect is not an issue as long as the two materials degrade homogeneously. However, in reality, the silicon and graphite parts of the anode often age differently, effectively creating a three-electrode problem during electrode balancing. Kunz et al. [23] also report the named challenges when reproducing the electrode balancing of aged cells with SiC anodes. Schmitt et al. [32] show the reconstruction of the differential voltage considering separate silicon and graphite anode particles. While mostly observed for SiC anodes, this effect might also come into play for cathodes, if the different materials separate partially into different metal-oxide particles with individual OCV curves.

In light of the presented challenges, the authors still expect the tested assumption that the entropy variation curves of the individual electrodes are unaffected by aging to be valid. As the prime source for the observed deviations, the OCV fitting procedure, specifically the missing consideration of an individual silicon component, as well as the heavy smearing of the differential OCV curves, must be considered. However, confirming this assumption requires further experimental evidence e.g. obtained through the characterization of the spatially-resolved degradation of the used cells conducted in the continuation of this study.

ce:list-item.ce:para: This study provides an extensive open source dataset that contains potentiometric entropy variation measurements on two cell types under various operating conditions.

The influence of SoC and load-history-dependent hysteresis align with the findings in literature. In contrast to other studies, no significant non-linearity of the OCV vs. temperature relationship was determined for the investigated temperature range.

The temperature-path-dependency previously reported in other studies was successfully reproduced. However, the present results do not verify the suggested hysteresis-type characteristic of the phenomenon.

The reconstruction of full cell EHC vs. SoC curves from half cell results was successfully conducted for pristine cells. For degraded cells, the reconstruction proved challenging, likely due to the severe degree of smearing of their OCV curves exacerbating the determination of the electrode balancing.

ce:section.ce:para: Beyond contributing to the general understanding of the characteristics and dependencies of entropy variation in lithium-ion batteries, the findings presented in this study further enable the design of meaningful entropy variation measurement campaigns under consideration of all relevant influences. In addition, the presented dataset allows the development of improved analysis methods without the need for conducting time-consuming experimental campaigns. Further research on the topic should focus on characterizing the temperature-path-dependency, particularly with regard to the conditions under which it becomes significant. Moreover, continuing investigations on the entropy variation of degraded full and half cells are necessary to fully understand the influence of aging on the entropy variation of Li-ion batteries.
